summary: The finite-difference quadratic form Q_n(p,q) := V_n(Omega^* oplus p oplus q) - V_n(Omega^* oplus p) - V_n(Omega^* oplus q) + V_n(Omega^*) measures the second-order excitation cost around the canonical vacuum.

name: Excitation cost
summary: The excitation cost lambda_n(p) := (Q_n(p,p), ||p||_n) is the lexicographic pair of the quadratic energy cost and the NF-norm, measuring how energetically expensive a perturbation is above the vacuum with degeneracy-breaking.

name: Canonical smallest excitation
summary: The canonical smallest excitation h_n is the lexicographic argmin of (lambda_n(p), code_n(p)) over all nontrivial localized perturbations p in P_n(U)\{0}, representing the lightest possible excitation above the vacuum at stage n.

name: Properties of h_n
summary: For each n >= n_0, h_n exists (P_n(U)\{0} is nonempty for sufficiently large n), is unique (lexicographic total order), and has strictly positive excitation cost Q_n(h_n, h_n) > 0.

name: Three kernel hypotheses
summary: The three kernel hypotheses for a tau-holonomy sector are: (KH-1) eventual stationarity of combinatorial type beyond a stabilization horizon n_*, (KH-2) refinement monotonicity of the defect functional, and (KH-3) strict positivity of Q_n(p,p) for all nontrivial p.

name: Verification status
summary: Verification status: (KH-1) follows from axiom K2 (profinite tower stabilizes), (KH-2) from NF monotonicity under restriction, and (KH-3) is substantive and must be verified per sector -- for Higgs see ch34, for Yang-Mills see ch41.

name: τ-Gap Meta-Theorem (III.T26)
summary: The tau-Gap Meta-Theorem (instantiating III.T26): for any tau-holonomy sector satisfying (KH-1)-(KH-3), the omega-vacuum exists by projective limit, the omega-gap quantum exists with positive spectral weight delta_S > 0, and the gap is non-decreasing along the tower.

name: Finite-stage spectral problem
summary: At each stage n >= n_0, Q_n is a symmetric non-negative bilinear form on R^{|P_n(U)|} with finite spectrum 0 = mu_0^(n) <= mu_1^(n) <= ... <= mu_{d_n}^(n), establishing the finite-dimensional spectral problem from which the gap is extracted.

name: Positive gap at each stage
summary: For each n >= n_* the positive gap mu_1^(n) > 0 holds: by (KH-3), Q_n(p,p) > 0 for all nontrivial p, and the minimum of a finite set of positive numbers is positive. In continuum QFT the analogous infimum need not be attained.

name: Vacuum coherence
summary: Vacuum coherence: for all n >= n_*, the restriction rho_{n+1->n}(Omega_{n+1}^*) = Omega_n^*, established by restriction preserving admissibility and optimality, paralleling the strong vacuum truncation coherence theorem.

name: Excitation coherence
summary: Excitation coherence: for all n >= n_*, rho(h_{n+1}) = h_n, because restriction maps perturbations to perturbations, excitation cost is non-decreasing under restriction by (KH-2), and surjectivity (KH-1) prevents strict improvement.

name: Why α-Idx prevents gap closure
summary: The discreteness of alpha-Idx (positive naturals, from the coherence kernel) prevents the gap from closing: a non-decreasing sequence of positive integers is eventually constant by (KH-1), so the gap stabilizes at a positive value and cannot shrink below 1.

name: Higgs sector as τ-holonomy sector
summary: The electroweak/Higgs sector defines a tau-holonomy sector S_wk by setting C_n to EM-null configurations on the base tau^1, admissible set to admissible weak connections, and defect functional to the weak curvature measure.

name: Higgs sector satisfies (KH-1)--(KH-3)
summary: The Higgs sector satisfies all three kernel hypotheses: (KH-1) stabilization at n_* = 2 (primorial depth of B), (KH-2) weak curvature defect is refinement-monotone, and (KH-3) discrete Hessian spectrum lemma gives lambda_1 > 0 after Goldstone absorption.

name: Strong sector as τ-holonomy sector
summary: The strong sector defines a tau-holonomy sector S_s via C_n = admissible strong connections G_n on the hadronic torus T(H)[n], with chi_minus-preservation and the strong defect functional Delta_n^s.

name: Strong sector kernel hypotheses
summary: Strong sector kernel hypotheses: (KH-1) stabilization at n_* = 3 (C-sector activation depth), (KH-2) Delta_n^s is refinement-monotone since L_s[n] subset L_s[n+1], and (KH-3) is deferred to ch41 requiring confinement and color holonomy.

name: Glueball mass scale
summary: Lattice QCD predicts the lightest glueball at approx 1.5-1.7 GeV (0^{++} quantum numbers); the tau-prediction will be derived in ch41 once (KH-3) is established and the spectral readout is performed. Scope: conjectural.

name: The Millennium Problem, revisited
summary: The Clay Millennium Problem asks for a mass gap in continuum Yang-Mills on R^4; in Category tau the question becomes whether the C-sector of B on T^2 has a positive first eigenvalue, answered yes subject to (KH-3) verification, but the bridge to continuum QFT is conjectural.

name: The simplest non-trivial case
summary: The Millennium Problem requires proof for any compact simple gauge group; the simplest non-trivial case is SU(2). Category tau addresses SU(3) directly since the C-sector naturally produces this gauge algebra, which is the physically most important case (QCD).

name: The lattice evidence
summary: Lattice simulations by Teper, Morningstar, et al. have computed glueball masses over four decades; the lightest SU(3) glueball has J^PC = 0^{++} and mass approx 1.7 GeV (sigma approx 0.18 GeV^2). The evidence is compelling; the proof is absent.

name: Strong configuration space
summary: The strong configuration space C_s[n] = Adm_s[n] / ~_{Gamma_s^*[n]} is the quotient of strongly admissible endomorphisms at stage n by the equivalence relation induced by the canonical strong vacuum, forming the space over which the mass gap is analyzed.

name: Strong connection assignment
summary: A strong connection assignment at stage n is a map Gamma_n: E(C_s[n]) -> Aut(T_n^col) assigning to each edge of the finite cell complex an automorphism of the color phase fiber, the tau-analogue of a lattice gauge configuration.

name: Analogy with lattice gauge theory
summary: The strong connection assignment is the tau-analogue of a lattice gauge configuration in lattice QCD, where one assigns U_ell in SU(3) to each link; the difference is that the cell complex comes from the boundary holonomy algebra, not a regular hypercubic lattice.

name: Strong curvature
summary: The strong curvature F_n^s(Box) at plaquette Box and stage n measures the norm of the ordered composition of connection automorphisms around an elementary closed path minus the identity, quantifying the failure of parallel transport to commute around Box.

name: Plaquette-aggregated strong defect
summary: The plaquette-aggregated strong defect V_n^s(Gamma_n) = Agg({F_n^s(Box) | Box in P_n^s}) is the canonical aggregation of strong curvatures over all plaquettes, the plaquette reformulation of the gap-loop defect functional suited to spectral analysis.

name: Equivalence of defect formulations
summary: The plaquette-aggregated defect V_n^s and the gap-loop defect Delta_n^s determine the same vacuum: argmin of V_n^s over Adm_s[n] equals Gamma_s^*[n], because every gap loop decomposes into plaquettes and the aggregations are equivalent.

name: Canonical strong vacuum, plaquette form
summary: The canonical strong vacuum in plaquette form is Gamma_s^*[n] := argmin over V_n^s-minimizers of code_n^s(Gamma_n), the NF-coded tie-breaking among plaquette-defect minimizers, equivalent to the gap-loop construction.

name: Refinement coherence
summary: Refinement coherence: the restriction map rho from C_s[n+1] to C_s[n] satisfies rho(Gamma_s^*[n+1]) = Gamma_s^*[n] for all n >= n_* = 3, the C-sector activation depth, ensuring the plaquette-form vacuum is consistent across stages.

name: Strong quadratic form
summary: The strong quadratic form Q_n^s(p,q) is the finite-difference second variation of V_n^s around the strong vacuum Gamma_s^*[n], analogous to the Hessian of the Yang-Mills action evaluated at the vacuum, whose eigenvalues determine the excitation spectrum.

name: Properties of Q_n^s
summary: Properties of Q_n^s: symmetric (Q_n^s(p,q) = Q_n^s(q,p)), non-negative with equality iff p is gauge-equivalent to the vacuum, finite rank (finite-dimensional configuration space), providing the spectral data for the mass gap.

name: Spectral gap at stage~n
summary: The spectral gap delta_n^s := lambda_1^(n) = min{lambda in Spec(Q_n^s) | lambda > 0} is the smallest nonzero eigenvalue of the strong quadratic form at stage n; the corresponding eigenmode g_n is the lightest excitation above the strong vacuum.

name: YM sector coupling
summary: The YM sector coupling mu_YM(k) is the ratio of the B-product to the C-product of the split-complex zeta function at primorial depth k (III.D46), measuring the degree of asymmetry between the two bipolar factorizations that controls the spectral gap scale.

name: Lean verification
summary: Lean verification: positivity of the YM sector coupling at depths 3-5 verified via native_decide (gap_pos_3, gap_grows_3_4, ym_gap_is_tau_gap_3) in TauLib.BookIII.Physics.GapTheorem, confirming the tau-gap is positive and non-decreasing.

name: Gap mode coherence
summary: Gap mode coherence: for all n >= 3 beyond stabilization, the restriction map satisfies rho(g_{n+1}) = g_n, ensuring the lightest excitation modes at successive stages are consistent and the projective limit g[omega] is well-defined.

name: Gap quantum
summary: The omega-gap quantum g[omega] := varprojlim_{n>=3} g_n is the projective limit of finite-stage gap modes; its spectral weight lambda_omega^s(g[omega]) := lim delta_n^s is the mass gap of the strong sector.

name: Gap positivity at each finite stage
summary: Gap positivity at each finite stage: for every n >= 3, delta_n^s > 0, because C_s[n] is finite, Q_n^s is positive-definite on non-vacuum perturbations, and the minimum of a finite set of positive eigenvalues is positive.

name: Tower monotonicity
summary: Tower monotonicity: delta_{n+1}^s >= delta_n^s for all n >= 3, so the spectral gap is non-decreasing along the refinement tower. Higher refinement strengthens admissibility constraints, increasing the energy cost of any excitation.

name: Physical interpretation
summary: Higher resolution makes the mass gap larger, not smaller: adding more modes strengthens admissibility constraints, increasing excitation energy cost. This contrasts with the continuum picture where more modes could fill the gap. Lean-verified through depth 5.

name: Profinite spectral preservation
summary: Profinite spectral preservation: the spectrum of the omega-limit quadratic form Q_omega^s has no eigenvalues in (0, delta_infinity^s), because the profinite limit does not introduce new eigenvalues that were absent at every finite stage.

name: The key structural point
summary: The proof relies on projective limits of discrete spectra not introducing new eigenvalues: in a continuum limit new eigenvalues can accumulate at zero, but in a profinite limit the spectrum grows monotonically and cannot fill in the gap.

name: No continuum parameter to tune
summary: In Category tau there is no continuously tunable parameter: the coupling kappa(C;3) = iota_tau^3/(1-iota_tau) is fixed by iota_tau. There is no knob to turn that could drive the gap to zero; the gap is positive because the structure requires it.

name: τ-Yang--Mills Mass Gap Theorem
summary: The tau-Yang-Mills Mass Gap Theorem: in the C-sector at E1 level, the strong vacuum Gamma_s^*[omega] has a positive spectral gap delta_infinity^s > 0, the gap mode g[omega] exists, and the gap is non-decreasing and spectrally isolated from below.

name: Physical meaning
summary: The tau-YM mass gap delta_infinity^s is the minimum excitation energy above the strong vacuum, corresponding in the orthodox readout to the mass of the lightest glueball (lattice QCD: approx 1.7 GeV, 0^{++} quantum numbers).

name: Readout functor (conjectural)
summary: The tau-to-orthodox readout functor R: Spec_tau(C) -> Spec_YM(SU(3)) is a conjectured map from the tau-spectrum of the C-sector to the physical spectrum of SU(3) Yang-Mills on R^4, required to preserve gaps, ordering, and multiplicities.

name: Orthodox Bridge Conjecture
summary: The Orthodox Bridge Conjecture asserts that a readout functor satisfying gap preservation, spectral ordering, and multiplicity conditions exists, so that the tau-mass gap delta_infinity^s > 0 implies a mass gap Delta > 0 in continuum SU(3) Yang-Mills on R^4.

name: Comparison with lattice QCD
summary: The relationship between tau and orthodox QFT is analogous to lattice QCD vs. continuum QCD: both provide rigorous finite-stage results, but the continuum/orthodox limit is taken by extrapolation, not by rigorous proof. The analogy is precise.

name: No claim to the prize
summary: No claim to the Clay Millennium Prize is made: the prize requires proof within orthodox QFT on R^4, which is a different framework. The tau-result demonstrates the mass gap as a structural consequence of the profinite tower; translation to orthodox language is open.

name: Why π-typed, not η-typed
summary: The strong coupling measures how the C-sector responds to base probes: a purely eta-typed probe sees only the vacuum, while a pi-typed probe enters through the crossing point omega, interacts with the strong vacuum, and returns with a measurable effect.

name: π-lift at stage n
summary: The pi-lift at stage n is Lift_pi(n), obtained by restricting the canonical strong lift Lift_{s,n} to pi-supported endomorphisms and selecting the NF-minimal element; it is the tau-analogue of a deep-inelastic scattering probe of the strong sector.

name: π-lift ω-limit
summary: The pi-lift omega-limit Lift_pi^omega := [(Lift_pi(n))_{n>=3}]_{~omega} in H_partial is the tail equivalence class of the finite-stage pi-lifts, representing the profinite strong coupling as a well-defined element of the boundary algebra.

name: The τ-strong coupling constant
summary: The tau-strong coupling constant alpha_s^* := NF(Lift_pi^omega) in Fix(s) is the normal-form selector applied to the pi-lift omega-limit, yielding a unique element of the boundary algebra determined entirely by the strong vacuum and pi-support constraint.

name: Uniqueness of the strong coupling
summary: Uniqueness of the strong coupling: any tau-admissible construction that preserves the strong vacuum, is pi-supported, and yields a fixed point of HolEnd_tau(s) must equal alpha_s^*. No alternative coupling construction is compatible with all three constraints.

name: No primitive mixing
summary: No primitive mixing: alpha_s^* is distinct from alpha_em^* and alpha_wk^*; no tau-admissible endomorphism intertwines Fix(s) with Fix(EM) or Fix(wk), because the fixed-point subalgebras intersect only at iota_tau (which is not a derived coupling).

name: Mixing at the cross-coupling level
summary: Cross-couplings between sectors do exist: kappa(B,C) = iota_tau^3/(1+iota_tau) approx 0.0297 is a structural invariant of the Higgs-crossing geometry. The no-mixing proposition concerns primitive self-couplings, not inter-sector invariants.

name: π-support penalty
summary: The pi-support penalty Pen_pi[n](x) := sum_{k=n+1}^{2n} ||x|_k - pr_pi(x|_k)||_{R[k]} measures how far an endomorphism deviates from pure pi-typed action at stages beyond n, penalizing non-pi-typed contamination in the strong coupling construction.

name: Lean translatability
summary: Each of the five recipe steps (loop code, admissibility, defect, pi-lift, omega-limit) involves only finite decidable operations on finitely presented rings; steps 1-4 are Decidable in Lean 4, step 5 is a Filter.Tendsto statement.

name: The argmin is the π-lift
summary: The argmin of the combined defect Delta_pi[n](x) over admissible pi-typed endomorphisms A_pi[n] equals the pi-lift Lift_pi(n) for all n >= 3, verifying that the canonical construction coincides with the variational characterization.

name: Range proof from Lean
summary: The Lean module CouplingFormulas proves 2*kappa(C;3) in (0.119, 0.122) via native_decide on 6-digit rational approximations of iota_tau, bracketing the PDG value alpha_s(M_Z) = 0.1180 with a 2.4% overshoot at leading order.

name: What varies
summary: In the orthodox picture the coupling varies with energy scale; in the tau picture the readout varies. The decomposition alpha_s(mu) = kappa(C;3) * R_C(mu^2) separates the fixed ontic coupling from the scale-dependent readout functor.

name: Λ_QCD as readout saturation
summary: Lambda_QCD is the energy at which the readout functor R_C(mu^2) ceases to be injective on the boundary algebra; below this scale multiple distinct boundary states project to the same measured value, and the readout loses information (readout saturation).

name: Ontic coupling
summary: An ontic coupling is an element of H_partial obtained by finite-stage minimization, omega-tail stabilization, and NF normalization, belonging to Fix(S) for some sector S. It is the fundamental, scale-independent quantity.

name: Regime selector
summary: A regime selector is a finite datum specifying a truncation depth n_0, an operational chart (coordinate choice on the readout target), a sector carrier choice, and a calibration bridge from tau-units to SI or MS-bar units.

name: Regime readout map
summary: The regime readout map Read_S[R]: H_partial -> R_R maps boundary algebra elements to real numbers in the metric of the regime's operational chart, extracting the measured value of an ontic coupling in a specific experimental context.

name: No ontic running --- strong sector
summary: No ontic running in the strong sector: alpha_s^* = kappa(C;3) is independent of all regime selectors (same element of H_partial), but different regime readouts can give different numerical values, reproducing the apparent energy-dependent alpha_s(mu).

name: Asymptotic freedom as spectral tightening
summary: Asymptotic freedom as spectral tightening: at high energy scales (mu >> Lambda_QCD), the C-sector readout satisfies R_C(mu^2) < 1 and decreases with increasing mu, because chi_minus-dominant characters tighten under deeper refinement.

name: The physical picture
summary: The orthodox explanation involves anti-screening by gluon self-interaction; the tau explanation is its spectral dual: chi_minus tightening concentrates the ontic color structure at deeper refinement stages, so a high-energy probe subsamples the concentrated structure.

name: Unification in the τ picture
summary: In orthodox GUTs the three gauge couplings approximately unify near 10^16 GeV; in Category tau the ontic couplings are different rational functions of iota_tau and were never meant to unify. EW "unification" is the inter-sector coupling kappa(A,B) = iota_tau^3.

name: The denominator matters
summary: The most significant improvement of the 2nd Edition formula alpha_s^* = kappa(C;3) = iota_tau^3/(1-iota_tau) is not the numerical value but the structural content: the (1-iota_tau) denominator simultaneously produces confinement, mass gap, and asymptotic freedom.

name: Quark mode
summary: A quark mode is a character mode chi_{m,n} on T^2 with eta-winding number n not equiv 0 mod 3, carrying color class c = n mod 3 in {1,2}; by the Confinement Theorem such modes cannot exist as isolated stable states.

name: Quark electric charges
summary: Quark electric charges are determined by color class: d-type quarks (n equiv 1 mod 3) have Q = -1/3 e, u-type quarks (n equiv 2 mod 3) have Q = +2/3 e, arising from the ternary eta-circle structure and the crossing coupling omega.

name: Fractional charges are not inputs
summary: In the Standard Model quark charges -1/3 and +2/3 are empirical inputs; in Category tau they are consequences of the ternary eta-circle structure and crossing coupling. The number 1/3 is the reciprocal of the color rank; 2/3 = 1 - 1/3 is its complement.

name: Antiquark mode
summary: The antiquark of a quark mode chi_{m,n} is the conjugate mode bar{chi}_{m,n} := chi_{-m,-n}, with reversed color class bar{c} = (-n) mod 3 and reversed electric charge -Q, corresponding to the CP-conjugate character on T^2.

name: Why only three, not more
summary: Exactly three topologically distinct mode classes exist on L = S^1 v S^1 because L has exactly three structurally distinct regions: crossing point, single lobe, and full figure. A bouquet of k circles would give k+1 classes, but the lemniscate has k=2, giving 3.

name: Quark generations from \Lemniscate
summary: The three quark generations arise from three topological mode classes on L: Generation 1 (u,d) = crossing-point modes, Generation 2 (c,s) = single-lobe modes, Generation 3 (t,b) = full-lemniscate modes, with increasing breathing eigenvalues.

name: Generation mass ordering
summary: Generation mass ordering: breathing eigenvalues satisfy lambda_crossing < lambda_single-lobe < lambda_full-L, yielding the mass hierarchies m_u < m_c < m_t and m_d < m_s < m_b. Scope: conjectural (quantitative spectrum deferred to Part VII).

name: Leptons share the same structure
summary: The three lepton generations (e, nu_e), (mu, nu_mu), (tau, nu_tau) are governed by the same lemniscate topology as quarks: electron = crossing-point, muon = single-lobe, tau = full-L, with identical mass hierarchy origin differing only in color quantum number.

name: Gluon count
summary: The strong sector admits exactly 8 independent connection modes corresponding to the 8 generators of su(3): dim_R su(3) = 3^2 - 1 = 8, each basis element corresponding to a gluon type as an independent gauge boson.

name: Why \mathrm{SU not \mathrm{U
summary: If the gauge group were U(3) instead of SU(3), there would be 9 gauge bosons including a color-neutral ninth "gluon" behaving like a second photon. The color-neutral vacuum forces tracelessness, earning SU(3) and structurally excluding the ninth boson.

name: Gluon self-interaction vertices
summary: The non-abelian field strength produces two gluon self-interaction vertices: a three-gluon vertex (proportional to g_s f_{abc}, from the commutator [A_mu, A_nu]) and a four-gluon vertex (proportional to g_s^2, quartic in the gauge field).

name: Self-interaction and confinement
summary: Gluon self-interaction means the gauge field itself carries color charge, so the field between quarks is a source of color, producing flux tubes whose energy grows linearly with separation -- qualitatively different from electrically neutral EM fields.

name: Structural asymptotic freedom
summary: Structural asymptotic freedom: the C-sector readout R_C(n) is monotonically decreasing with refinement depth n, so the effective strong coupling alpha_s^eff(n) = kappa(C;3) * R_C(n) decreases at higher resolution, directly from chi_minus tightening.

name: The χ₋/χ₊ duality
summary: The four fiber-and-base forces exhibit a clean polarity pattern: B(EM) is chi_plus (screening, Landau pole), C(Strong) is chi_minus (anti-screening, asymptotic freedom), A(Weak) is balanced (parity violation), D(Gravity) is chi_plus (field-strength dependent).

name: Asymptotic freedom from N_c and N_f
summary: The tau-derived values N_c = 3 and N_f = 6 satisfy the asymptotic freedom condition N_f = 6 < 11*N_c/2 = 16.5, confirming asymptotic freedom at one loop both by the orthodox beta function and the chi_minus-tightening structural argument.

name: Meson state
summary: A meson is a composite state |q bar{q}> formed from a quark mode chi_{m1,n1} and an antiquark mode chi_{-m2,-n2} such that the total color charge c_total = n1 mod 3 + (-n2) mod 3 equiv 0 mod 3 vanishes, making it a color singlet.

name: Baryon state
summary: A baryon is a composite state |q_r q_g q_b> formed from three quark modes with pairwise distinct color charges {n1 mod 3, n2 mod 3, n3 mod 3} = {0,1,2}, so the total color charge (n1+n2+n3) mod 3 = 0, carrying baryon number B = 1.

name: Neutron stability in nuclei
summary: While the free neutron is unstable (half-life approx 10 min via A-sector beta decay), neutrons bound in nuclei are stable because nuclear binding energy lowers the effective mass below the decay threshold; full analysis requires the hadron classification of Part VII.

name: The worst prediction
summary: The vacuum catastrophe is a 10^120 discrepancy between the theoretical QFT vacuum energy and the observed cosmological constant -- the worst prediction in the history of physics, with no other discrepancy in natural science approaching this magnitude.

name: Boundary-first normalization
summary: A physical quantity Q satisfies boundary-first normalization if Q = eval composed with chi composed with omega-germ, factoring through the profinite omega-germ limit, a boundary character (tail-invariant endomorphism), and evaluation to tau-Idx.

name: No uncountable factorization
summary: No uncountable factorization: any physical quantity satisfying boundary-first normalization involves only finite sums at each stage, the profinite omega-germ limit, and evaluation in tau-Idx; no uncountable sum, continuum integral, or UV-divergent operation appears.

name: The key distinction
summary: The orthodox derivation begins with uncountably many Fock space degrees of freedom summed with equal standing; in Category tau, the Fock space is replaced by the profinite refinement tower, countable at every stage and compact in the profinite topology.

name: Canonical vacuum uniqueness
summary: Each of the four sector vacua (Omega^*_EM, Omega^*_wk, Gamma^*_s, nabla^*_GR) is the unique minimizer of its sector's defect functional over sector-admissible endomorphisms, not a representative of a shift equivalence class. No additive freedom remains.

name: No vacuum energy offset
summary: In orthodox QFT one chooses to set <0|H|0> = 0 by normal ordering, introducing a free vacuum energy offset; in Category tau the vacuum energy is a readout of the defect functional at the canonical minimizer -- not a choice but a structural consequence.

name: Earned vs.\ unearned mode count
summary: An earned mode count N_n = dim(H_partial[n]|_{T^2}) < infinity is the number of independent boundary characters on T^2 at stage n; an unearned mode count is any infinite cardinal assigned to continuum degrees of freedom without profinite justification.

name: No vacuum catastrophe in τ
summary: No vacuum catastrophe in tau: the tau-vacuum energy density rho_vac^(tau) = sum over {B,A,C,D} of eval(Delta^S_omega(Vac^*_S)) is finite (a stabilized boundary invariant), parameter-free (no additive offset), and structurally prevented from diverging.

name: Largest but finite
summary: The strong sector contributes the most to the vacuum energy, but the geometric series sum iota_tau^k = 1/(1-iota_tau) approx 1.518 converges; the non-truncating accumulation involves all stages but does not produce a divergence.

name: Tail stabilization of vacuum energy
summary: Tail stabilization of vacuum energy: there exists a stabilization horizon N_s such that VacE_s[n+1] = VacE_s[n] for all n >= N_s, so the omega-germ limit VacE_s[omega] = VacE_s[N_s] is a finite element of tau-Idx.

name: Global stabilization
summary: Global stabilization: each of the four sectors has a stabilization horizon N_B, N_A, N_C, N_D, and the total vacuum energy rho_vac^(tau) = sum VacE_S[omega] stabilizes beyond N^* = max(N_B, N_A, N_C, N_D).

name: No double counting
summary: The sector decomposition H_partial[omega] = direct sum over {B,A,C,D,omega} is direct; the crossing sector omega mediates between B and C but does not carry an independent vacuum. The total vacuum energy sums exactly four terms with no double counting.

name: Open problem: numerical value
summary: The vacuum catastrophe is resolved (the 10^120 discrepancy does not arise), but the numerical value of rho_vac^(tau) has not been computed to experimental precision; this requires explicit evaluation of all four sector defect functionals at their stabilization horizons.

name: Book III template vs Book IV instantiation
summary: Book III established the sector decomposition as a mathematical template on L with coupling ledger and No Knobs Theorem (III.T42); the 13-row sector atlas table in ch45 is the physical E1 instantiation of that abstract template, filling every entry with derived (not postulated) physical content across all five sectors.

name: Exactly four primitive forces (physical reading)
summary: The boundary holonomy algebra H_partial[omega] admits exactly four linearly independent primitive sector characters, instantiating at E1 as Gravity (D), Weak (A), EM (B), and Strong (C); no fifth primitive sector exists and no GUT unification reduces this count.

name: Exactly one derived sector
summary: The lemniscate L = S^1 v S^1 has exactly one self-intersection point, so the sector decomposition admits exactly one derived sector omega = B cap C = gamma cap eta; no other pair of primitive sectors produces a derived sector.

name: Topological rigidity
summary: The Exactly-One-Derived Theorem is topologically rigid: any homeomorphism preserving the S^1 v S^1 structure preserves the unique wedge point, so the omega-sector (Higgs mechanism) cannot be removed by continuous deformation nor can a second crossing be created.

name: 9-element canonical generator set
summary: The canonical generator set of H_partial[omega] consists of nine elements in three groups: four sector vacuum idempotents (gravitational, weak, EM, strong), four gap quanta (one per sector), and the crossing generator iota_tau coupling chi_+ and chi_- at the lemniscate crossing point.

name: Generator adequacy and minimality
summary: The nine elements of the canonical generator set generate the entire boundary holonomy algebra H_partial[omega], and no proper subset suffices; every polynomial expression in these nine generators yields a physical observable, establishing both adequacy and minimality.

name: tau-Yukawa coupling
summary: The tau-Yukawa coupling of a fermion mode chi_{m,n} in sector X to the Higgs sector omega is y_f = kappa(omega) / sqrt(m^2 + n^2 iota_tau^2) times a generation factor Gamma_gen(f), determined by winding-mode overlap with the omega-sector crossing character.

name: Yukawa as readout not parameter
summary: The Yukawa hierarchy spanning six orders of magnitude from y_e to y_t is a readout of winding-mode geometry on T^2, not a set of independent parameters; it arises from compounding three geometric factors each determined by iota_tau alone, making it a prediction rather than a problem.

name: SM parameter count comparison
summary: The Standard Model requires approximately 19 free parameters; Category tau requires 9 canonical generators of which only one (iota_tau) is a numerical constant, the remaining eight being structural objects uniquely determined by the boundary algebra --- a reduction from 19 free parameters to 1 master constant via the No Knobs Principle.

name: No BSM particles
summary: The periodic table of tau-particles contains no beyond-Standard-Model particles: no supersymmetric partners, no leptoquarks, no right-handed neutrinos, no fourth generation, and no dark matter candidates with new quantum numbers, as a structural consequence of the Exactly-Four Theorem and lemniscate topology.

name: Three mode classes on lemniscate
summary: Character modes on T^2 restricted to L = S^1 v S^1 fall into three topological mode classes: crossing-point modes (Generation 1, localized near p_omega, smallest eigenvalue), single-lobe modes (Generation 2, winding around one lobe), and full-lemniscate modes (Generation 3, winding around both lobes, largest eigenvalue).

name: Exactly three generations
summary: The lemniscate L = S^1 v S^1 supports exactly three topologically distinct mode classes (crossing-point, single-lobe, full-lemniscate) and no fourth class exists, because L has exactly three structurally distinct regions: the crossing point, a single lobe, and the full figure.

name: Fourth generation excluded
summary: The exclusion of a fourth generation is topological, not energetic: L = S^1 v S^1 has exactly two lobes and one crossing point, so no fourth mode class exists regardless of energy; the LEP measurement N_nu = 2.9840 +/- 0.0082 confirms this topological prediction.

name: Winding number classes
summary: In the character lattice Lambda_CR = Z^2 of T^2, the three mode classes correspond to three winding regions: crossing-point modes have low winding (m,0) or (0,n), single-lobe modes wind around one circle, and full-lemniscate modes have non-trivial winding (m,n) around both circles.

name: Quark winding classes
summary: The six quarks are assigned to three winding classes on T^2 with shape ratio iota_tau: Class (1,0) for Generation 1 (u, d) with eigenvalue beta = 1, Class (0,1) for Generation 2 (c, s) with eigenvalue beta = iota_tau^(-1), and Class (1,1) for Generation 3 (t, b) with eigenvalue beta = iota_tau^(-2).

name: Quark mass pattern
summary: The six quark masses follow approximate iota_tau power laws relative to m_n: m_u ~ m_n iota_tau^5.8, m_d ~ m_n iota_tau^5.0, m_c ~ m_n iota_tau^(-0.30), m_s ~ m_n iota_tau^2.2, m_t ~ m_n iota_tau^(-4.5), m_b ~ m_n iota_tau^(-1.5); scope is conjectural, reproducing ordering and scale but not high-precision values.

name: Honest assessment of quark masses
summary: Honest assessment: the quark mass predictions correctly reproduce mass ordering within each generation (m_d > m_u, m_s < m_c, m_b < m_t), the generation hierarchy, and the scale of each generation (MeV, GeV, hundreds of GeV), but are structural pattern matches, not digit-level predictions.

name: Muon mass exponent
summary: The muon-to-electron mass ratio is m_mu/m_e = iota_tau^(-5)(1 + delta_mu) where delta_mu ~ -0.04 is an O(alpha) radiative correction; the bare topological exponent is 5, and the corrected prediction m_e iota_tau^(-4.96) ~ 106.1 MeV matches experiment (105.66 MeV) to 0.4%.

name: Non-integer exponents are physical
summary: Generation exponents are not exact integers: bare topological values (5 for muon, 15/2 for tau lepton) are shifted by electromagnetic radiative corrections of order alpha ~ 1/137, following the same pattern as the neutron mass ratio where the bare exponent 7 receives an O(alpha^2) holonomy correction.

name: Tau lepton mass exponent
summary: The tau-to-electron mass ratio is m_tau/m_e = iota_tau^(-15/2)(1 + delta_tau) where delta_tau ~ +0.09 is an O(alpha) radiative correction; the bare topological exponent is 15/2 = 7.5, and the full-lemniscate winding mode produces the heaviest charged lepton.

name: Koide parameter
summary: The Koide parameter is Q = (m_e + m_mu + m_tau) / (sqrt(m_e) + sqrt(m_mu) + sqrt(m_tau))^2, a dimensionless ratio of the three charged lepton masses discovered by Yoshio Koide in 1982, experimentally equal to 0.666661 +/- 0.000007.

name: Koide relation Q=2/3
summary: The three charged lepton masses satisfy Q = 2/3 to all orders in the lemniscate topology; the proof uses the Z/3Z symmetry of L's three sectors (crossing, lobe 1, lobe 2) with 120-degree democratic matrix spacing, yielding Q = 2/3 independent of mass scale or Koide phase, with deviation O(alpha^2) ~ 5e-5.

name: The 45-degree crossing angle
summary: The Koide phase delta is not arbitrary but determined by the lemniscate crossing angle: the standard lemniscate r^2 = a^2 cos(2theta) has crossing angle exactly 45 degrees = pi/4; the phase delta = pi/4 - pi/12 = pi/6 determines m_mu/m_e but not Q, which is 2/3 for all delta.

name: Why 0.0009% and not exact
summary: The experimental deviation Q_exp - 2/3 = -6e-6 is of order alpha^2 ~ 5e-5, consistent with radiative correction from EM vacuum polarization (the same mechanism producing the pi^3 alpha^2 correction in the R formula); the Koide relation is exact at tree level within Category tau.

name: Neutrino mass scale
summary: The heaviest neutrino mass eigenstate satisfies m_3(nu) ~ m_e iota_tau^15 ~ 50.7 meV; the exponent 15 = dim(τ³)×|generators| = 3×5 (Wave 11: generator orbit derivation). CP asymmetry from A-sector polarity ε_CP = ι_τ connects neutrino mass scale to baryogenesis. Scope: conjectural (exponent 15 interpretation strengthened but not fully derived).

name: Normal ordering predicted
summary: The tau-framework predicts normal neutrino mass ordering: the lightest eigenstate is the Generation 1 crossing-point mode, the heaviest is the Generation 3 full-lemniscate mode, following the same breathing eigenvalue hierarchy as charged leptons; testable by JUNO, DUNE, and Hyper-Kamiokande.

name: Individual neutrino masses
summary: The formula m_3(nu) ~ m_e iota_tau^15 predicts the heaviest eigenstate; individual masses m_1, m_2, m_3 require the full generation structure on base tau^1 with a Koide-like parametrization using a different phase delta_nu reflecting A-sector rather than B-sector coupling, deferred to Part VII.

name: Scope gradient across mass table
summary: The mass table exhibits a clear scope gradient: tau-effective established (m_e at 0.025 ppm, massless photon/gluon), tau-effective structural (Koide Q = 2/3, three generations, massless gauge bosons), tau-effective approximate (muon/tau exponents, neutrino scale), and conjectural (quark masses, W/Z/H masses).

name: One constant one anchor zero parameters
summary: Every fundamental particle mass in the table is determined by one dimensionless constant iota_tau = 2/(pi+e), one dimensional anchor m_n = 939.565421 MeV, and zero free dimensionless parameters; the Standard Model's ~25 free parameters (Yukawas, gauge couplings, CKM/PMNS, Higgs VEV, QCD angle) reduce to this single input.

name: Neutron as parent of atomic matter
summary: In the tau-picture, beta-decay is not destruction of a neutron but differentiation of the calibration anchor into its component spectral modes: proton (weak-polarized neutron, p = n + delta_A), electron (spectral residual m_e = m_n/R), and antineutrino (base-mode time-eigenstate); the neutron is the parent of all atomic matter.

name: Beta-decay Q-value
summary: The beta-decay Q-value Q_beta = (delta_A - m_e)c^2 = (1.293 - 0.511) MeV ~ 0.782 MeV, with both delta_A and m_e determined by iota_tau through the mass ratio chain; the experimental value Q_beta = 0.782333(4) MeV confirms sub-percent agreement.

name: Structural lifetime estimate
summary: The neutron lifetime formula involves G_F (from electroweak arc), m_e (from mass ratio), Q_beta (from proton-neutron mass difference), all iota_tau-determined. With g_A at +5.5 ppm (IV.T182) and the cancelled form (77 ppm RSS), the structural estimate is promoted to tau-effective. Scope upgraded from conjectural at Wave 46B.

name: Bohr radius from iota_tau
summary: The Bohr radius a_0 = R/(alpha_em m_n) (hbar/c) is fully determined by iota_tau and m_n, since both R = m_n/m_e (from the 10-link chain) and alpha_em ~ (8/15) iota_tau^4 are rational functions of iota_tau; the prediction matches the CODATA value a_0 ~ 5.292e-11 m.

name: No classical trajectory
summary: The electron does not travel along a classical trajectory; it occupies a holomorphic state psi in H_tau on fiber T^2, anchored to the proton's defect bundle by B-sector EM coupling, with the Bohr radius a_0 as the characteristic scale of this holomorphic state, not a classical orbit radius.

name: Hydrogen energy levels
summary: The hydrogen bound-state energies E_n = -alpha_em^2 m_e c^2 / (2n^2) = -13.6 eV/n^2 for n = 1,2,3,... are eigenvalues of the B-sector Hamiltonian restricted to the proton-electron system, with both alpha_em and m_e determined by iota_tau, yielding ground-state energy E_1 ~ -13.606 eV.

name: Rydberg constant
summary: The Rydberg constant R_infinity = alpha_em^2 m_e c / (2 hbar) encodes the hydrogen energy levels via E_n = -hcR_infinity/n^2; it is a derived quantity in Category tau since both alpha_em and m_e are iota_tau-determined, making it a parameter-free prediction.

name: Rydberg prediction at 0.025 ppm
summary: The tau-predicted Rydberg constant R_infinity^(tau) ~ 10,973,731.29 m^(-1) compared with CODATA R_infinity = 10,973,731.568157(12) m^(-1) gives relative deviation ~0.026 ppm, inheriting the 0.025 ppm precision of the electron mass prediction.

name: A testable prediction
summary: The Rydberg constant is one of the most precisely measured quantities (uncertainty 1.1e-12), and the tau-prediction matches to 0.026 ppm (seven significant figures), limited by the 0.025 ppm residual in m_e; improvement requires either a Level 2 correction to the mass ratio or a more precise neutron mass measurement.

name: Spectral transition as mode-switching
summary: Each hydrogen spectral transition |n_2,l_2,m_2> to |n_1,l_1,m_1> is a CR-address mode-switching event on T^2: the initial and final CR-address states have winding numbers compatible with the respective quantum numbers, and the emitted photon carries energy Delta E and angular momentum Delta l = +/-1.

name: Forbidden transitions
summary: Transitions with Delta l = 0 (e.g., 2s to 1s) are "forbidden" because the winding number difference cannot be absorbed by a single photon mode; they require higher-order holonomy corrections (quadrupole or magnetic dipole), suppressed by higher powers of alpha_em corresponding to higher-order effects on T^2.

name: Fine structure from holonomy corrections
summary: The fine-structure splitting of hydrogen is a higher-order B-sector holonomy correction on T^2 of order alpha_em^4 m_e c^2 ~ 1.8e-4 eV, entirely determined by iota_tau; the n=2 level splits into j=3/2 and j=1/2 components, with the splitting computed from alpha_em ~ (8/15) iota_tau^4 and m_e = m_n/R(iota_tau).

name: Lamb shift in tau-framework
summary: The Lamb shift (~1057.845 MHz) is an alpha_em^5 m_e c^2 effect arising from vacuum breathing corrections: the electron's boundary character on L interacts with virtual EM vacuum modes, shifting s-state energies relative to p-states; in tau-language this is a conjectural-scope fifth-order holonomy effect on T^2.

name: All roads lead through m_e
summary: Every atomic-scale prediction factors through m_e = m_n/R(iota_tau): a_0 ~ 1/m_e, E_1 ~ m_e, R_infinity ~ m_e, lambda ~ 1/m_e; the 0.025 ppm precision of m_e is the ceiling for all atomic predictions, improvable only through Level 2 mass ratio corrections or better neutron mass measurements.

name: Meson classification
summary: A meson |q_i q_bar_j> is classified by three attributes: flavor content (quark flavors from {u,d,s,c,b,t}), spin-parity J^PC (from orbital and spin angular momentum), and generation class (lemniscate mode classes of constituent quarks); pseudoscalar (J=0) and vector (J=1) mesons correspond to anti-aligned and aligned spins.

name: Eta-eta prime splitting
summary: The eta-eta' mass splitting (~410 MeV) exceeds what quark masses alone can explain; the resolution is the axial anomaly breaking U(1)_A symmetry via quantum effects (instantons in orthodox QCD, defect-functional topology in Category tau), giving the eta' its anomalously large mass.

name: Glueball
summary: A glueball is a bound state of the strong vacuum field Gamma_s^* with no quark content: a color-neutral excitation of the su(3) connection field above the vacuum minimum, carrying positive mass at or above the Yang-Mills mass gap m_gap, predicted by lattice QCD at ~1.5-1.7 GeV for J^PC = 0^++.

name: Glueballs and the mass gap
summary: Glueball existence is a direct consequence of the mass gap: without a gap there would be massless gluonic excitations, but confinement ensures every excitation above vacuum carries positive mass; the lightest glueball at ~1.5 GeV satisfies m_gb ~ 2pi/sigma_tau^(1/2), though experimental identification is complicated by mixing with nearby scalar mesons.

name: Nucleon mass decomposition
summary: The nucleon mass decomposes as m_N = E_vac + E_kin + sum m_qi, where C-sector vacuum energy (~400 MeV), quark kinetic energy (~500 MeV), and bare quark rest masses (~12 MeV) contribute roughly 42%, 57%, and 1% respectively; the vacuum and kinetic terms account for ~99% of nucleon mass.

name: Mass from nothing
summary: The nucleon mass decomposition makes vivid E = mc^2: the nucleon's mass is overwhelmingly not the mass of its parts but the energy cost of confining them; in the tau-framework this is a structural consequence of the C-sector's non-truncating denominator (1 - iota_tau), whose infinite geometric series stores macroscopic energy.

name: Isospin splitting from polarity
summary: The inequality m_d > m_u has a structural origin: the d-quark has eta-winding n = 1 mod 3 (chi_- dominant sector of the color group) while the u-quark has n = 2 mod 3 (complement class); chi_- dominant coupling to the strong vacuum Gamma_s^* through tightening produces higher effective mass, yielding isospin splitting delta_A ~ 1.293 MeV.

name: Proton lighter but ontologically later
summary: The proton is lighter than the neutron yet ontologically derived: ontological priority in Category tau follows structural simplicity, not mass ordering; the neutron is unpolarized (zero charge, balanced sectors) while the proton has acquired B-sector charge +e and A-sector isospin projection --- lighter does not mean simpler.

name: Nuclear force
summary: The nuclear force between two nucleons is the residual C-sector interaction from partial overlap of their internal color fields at separations ~1 fm; at E1 readout it is mediated by virtual meson exchange, principally the pion (lightest meson, range ~ hbar/m_pi c ~ 1.4 fm).

name: Deuteron binding in tau-language
summary: The deuteron is the minimal two-nucleon winding configuration on T^2: the proton (uud) and neutron (udd) defect bundles share a common C-sector vacuum region, and eta-holonomy field overlap produces binding energy B_d ~ 2.224 MeV, a small defect-functional energy reduction from the overlap.

name: Nuclear force saturation
summary: Nuclear force saturation arises because T^2-winding constraints mediating the residual strong interaction are local: each nucleon's defect bundle has finite angular extent Delta theta ~ iota_tau on the eta-circle, so only ~12 nearest neighbors interact, causing binding energy per nucleon B/A to plateau near 8.8 MeV for large A.

name: Nuclear shell structure
summary: T^2-winding modes for nucleons in a nuclear potential organize into shells of approximately equal breathing eigenvalues; cumulative nucleon counts at shell closures produce the magic numbers (2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126), paralleling the electron-shell story with nuclear-scale energy gaps.

name: Spin-orbit from omega-sector
summary: The nuclear spin-orbit interaction (Goeppert Mayer and Jensen, 1949) arises in the tau-framework from the omega-sector: the crossing point p_omega = gamma cap eta couples angular momentum quantum numbers from T^2's two circles, producing a term proportional to kappa(omega) l dot s that splits high-winding shells to yield magic numbers 28, 50, 82, 126.

name: Why He-4 is tightly bound
summary: The alpha-particle (He-4, Z=N=2) is the first doubly magic nucleus: two protons and two neutrons each fill the lowest winding mode (0,0) with both spin orientations, completely filling all four nucleon slots; the large gap to the next shell makes it exceptionally bound (B/A ~ 7.1 MeV) and the preferred alpha-decay product.

name: Iron peak from competing sectors
summary: The binding energy maximum at A ~ 56 (iron peak) results from optimal balance between C-sector (strong) binding per nucleon (saturating) and B-sector (electromagnetic) repulsion per nucleon (growing as Z^2/A^(1/3)); the crossover where Coulomb cost exceeds marginal binding gain occurs near iron-56.

name: Nucleosynthesis forward to Book V
summary: The binding energy curve determines which nuclear reactions release or absorb energy: fusion releases energy below the iron peak (A < 56, powering main-sequence stars), fission releases energy above it (A > 56); the astrophysical consequences (nucleosynthesis, stellar evolution) are deferred to Book V.

name: Alpha-decay as mode cluster ejection
summary: Alpha-decay is the ejection of a completely filled lowest-winding-mode cluster from the parent nucleus: the four nucleons occupying the (0,0) mode on T^2 detach as a unit because their internal binding (B/A ~ 7.1 MeV for He-4) exceeds the residual nuclear attraction, tunneling through the Coulomb barrier.

name: Neutron stability inside nuclei
summary: Free neutrons decay in ~10 minutes, but neutrons inside most nuclei are stable because nuclear binding lowers the neutron's effective mass, making Q_beta = (m_n - m_p - m_e)c^2 - B_n + B_p negative; when Q_beta < 0 the decay is energetically forbidden --- a readout-level phenomenon, the ontic neutron being unchanged.

name: Gamma-decay as mode transition
summary: Gamma-decay is the same mechanism as atomic spectral lines: a transition between two winding modes on T^2 with the energy difference carried by a B-sector photon; the only difference is scale (nuclear transitions at ~MeV vs. atomic transitions at ~eV), the structural mechanism being identical.

name: Decay channels from sector admissibility
summary: Every radioactive decay satisfies sector admissibility constraints: color neutrality preserved (total eta-holonomy trivial mod 3), baryon number conserved, electric charge conserved (total gamma-holonomy), and energy-momentum conserved; each decay type (alpha, beta, gamma) corresponds to a specific sector-transition pattern on T^2.

name: Atom as dressed nuclear mode
summary: An atom of atomic number Z is a stable composite of a nuclear core (bound state of Z protons and N neutrons) and an electron shell (Z electron winding modes on T^2 bound by B-sector EM coupling); the neutral atom has vanishing net B-sector winding, making it electromagnetically closed.

name: Electron quantum numbers
summary: An electron mode on T^2 bound to a nuclear core of charge Z carries four quantum numbers: principal n (total winding depth, breathing operator eigenvalue tier), angular l = 0,...,n-1 (lobe structure on L), magnetic m_l = -l,...,+l (orientation on L), and spin m_s = +/-1/2 (chirality on T^2).

name: Madelung rule from T^2 geometry
summary: The Madelung rule (subshells fill in order of increasing n+l) has no first-principles derivation in orthodox physics; in Category tau, the breathing eigenvalue on T^2 with shape ratio iota_tau is E_{n,l} ~ -1/(n + l iota_tau)^2, and since iota_tau ~ 0.34, the n+l ordering emerges naturally from the fiber geometry.

name: Period length sequence
summary: The periodic table period lengths follow the pattern 2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32, 32,... where each length equals twice a perfect square and each value (except 2) appears twice; this is a topological consequence of T^2 fiber geometry: shell capacity 2n^2 combined with the Aufbau filling order determines noble gas closures.

name: Topological not accidental
summary: The sequence 2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32,... is a topological invariant of T^2: shell capacities 2n^2 follow from winding mode counting and period pairing follows from the Madelung energy ordering determined by iota_tau; the periodic table has its shape because the fiber torus has its shape.

name: Covalent bond
summary: A covalent bond of order k between atoms A and B is a set of k electron winding modes on T^2 that contribute simultaneously to the shell closure of both atoms: k=1 (single bond, e.g., H_2), k=2 (double bond, e.g., O=O), k=3 (triple bond, e.g., N triple-bond N); bond strength increases with k via mode-overlap integrals.

name: Molecules as mode-sharing graphs
summary: A molecule is a graph whose vertices are atomic nuclei and whose edges are covalent bonds weighted by order; chemical reactions are graph transformations --- a rewiring of mode-sharing patterns on T^2, providing a structural (metaphorical-scope) picture of chemistry as fiber-mode combinatorics.

name: Ionic bond
summary: An ionic bond is a complete transfer of electron winding modes from atom A to atom B such that both ions achieve or approach noble gas closure; the bond energy is the Coulomb interaction E_ionic ~ -k^2 alpha / r_AB, where alpha is the fine-structure constant and r_AB the inter-nuclear separation.

name: Metallic bond
summary: A metallic bond is a collective binding mode in which outermost electron winding modes are delocalized across the lattice (the T^2-winding analogue of the electron sea); delocalization explains conductivity (modes propagate under fields), malleability, and luster, arising in elements with few outer-shell electrons.

name: No new parameters for chemistry
summary: Every bond type (covalent, ionic, metallic, hydrogen, van der Waals) is a different pattern of B-sector winding modes on T^2; alpha derived from iota_tau sets the electromagnetic energy scale, and specific bond energies depend on mode-overlap geometry, but no new coupling constants are introduced beyond the one master constant.

name: Mode-repulsion geometry
summary: Molecular geometry follows from mode-repulsion: k winding-mode pairs on T^2 maximize minimum angular separation, yielding k=2 linear (180 deg), k=3 trigonal planar (120 deg), k=4 tetrahedral (109.5 deg), k=5 trigonal bipyramidal, k=6 octahedral; the symmetry depends only on k, not on iota_tau.

name: Hybrid modes
summary: A hybrid mode is a linear combination of s-type (l=0) and p-type (l=1) winding modes optimized for directional bonding: sp^3 yields four tetrahedral hybrids (109.5 deg, e.g., CH_4), sp^2 yields three planar hybrids plus one perpendicular p-mode (120 deg, e.g., C_2H_4), sp yields two linear hybrids (180 deg).

name: Homochirality and parity violation
summary: Homochirality of terrestrial life (L-amino acids, D-sugars) has a structural origin: the crossing generator omega couples the two T^2 orientations (gamma,eta) vs (eta,gamma) with asymmetry inherited from A-sector parity violation, providing a tiny energetic enantiomer preference amplified over geological time by autocatalytic feedback.

name: Structural vs quantitative chemistry
summary: Molecular geometry results span two scope levels: tau-effective (discrete hybridization types, VSEPR geometry table, chirality from T^2 orientation) and metaphorical (precise bond angles, quantitative energies, detailed potential energy surfaces requiring full multi-electron breathing computation on T^2 beyond simplest cases).

name: The donut ladder
summary: The donut ladder is the five-rung compositional sequence on T^2: (1) quarks (fractional-winding C-sector modes, confined), (2) hadrons and nuclei (color-neutral composites with magic-number shells), (3) atoms (nuclear cores dressed by electron shells), (4) molecules (mode-sharing covalent/ionic bonds), (5) bulk matter (collective modes).

name: Comparison with orthodox physics
summary: In orthodox physics the five rungs (QCD, nuclear, atomic, quantum chemistry, condensed matter) are separate disciplines with separate formalisms and effective parameters; in Category tau all five are one continuous ascent on T^2 with derived scale separations kappa(C) >> kappa(B) >> kappa(D) from a single master constant.

name: Parameter count
summary: Across all twenty-five results of Part VI, the only inputs are iota_tau = 2/(pi+e) derived from axioms K0-K6 and m_n = 939.565421 MeV as the single dimensional anchor; no fitting parameter, effective coupling, or ad hoc mass ratio is introduced at any point in the derivation.

name: Ontic entity
summary: An entity is ontic in Category tau if it satisfies at least one of: well-defined mode on fiber T^2 (particle), well-defined mode on base tau^1 (temporal/gravitational), well-defined crossing mode at omega = gamma cap eta (Higgs-type), or finite composite of ontic modes (hadrons, nuclei, atoms).

name: Ontic entities list
summary: The complete ontic register lists all tau-objects constructed in Book IV: neutron (anchor), proton (stable core), electron (lowest breathing eigenvalue), photon (null transport), gluon (C-sector connection), W/Z bosons (A-sector gap quanta), Higgs (omega-sector scalar), neutrinos, quarks --- each with an explicit T^2 or tau^1 construction.

name: Non-ontic entities list
summary: Non-ontic entities (computational devices useful in orthodox calculations but not representing tau^3 objects): wave function psi as independent object (it is a character distribution, not a thing), virtual particles (internal Feynman diagram lines), path integral measure, renormalization group flow, and gravitons as particles.

name: Where the ontological line falls
summary: The ontic/non-ontic distinction is not philosophical preference but a mathematical consequence of the tau^3 fibration: an entity is ontic iff it can be constructed as a mode, character, or finite composite on tau^3 = tau^1 x_f T^2; this resolves longstanding questions about the reality of the wave function, virtual particles, and spacetime.

name: Dictionary limits
summary: The tau-to-SM translation dictionary covers sector decomposition, coupling ledger, QM infrastructure, and particle content but has limits: virtual particles, path integral measure, renormalization group flow, and gravitons as particles have no tau-counterpart; conversely, H_partial[omega], the breathing operator, and the defect functional have no SM counterpart.

name: Temperature is not fundamental
summary: Temperature is not fundamental in Category tau but a readout: the gradient of the defect functional with respect to the entropy component; Part VII will use the defect functional as organizing variable with temperature as derived quantity, mirroring Part III's reversal from wave function as fundamental to character distribution as readout.

name: Topological integrality of theta
summary: The topological charge theta(d) of a defect configuration on T^2 is integer-valued (winding number in pi_1(T^2) = Z^2) and is a deformation invariant preserved under continuous deformations within the boundary Hilbert space H_partial[omega].

name: Euler budget recap
summary: Recalls the Euler budget conservation mu + nu + kappa + theta = const from ch07 for single defect bundles in the inviscid regime, and poses the central question of ch52: what happens when this budget is aggregated over ~10^{23} bundles.

name: Macroscopic defect tuple
summary: The macroscopic defect tuple in sector X for an N-bundle configuration C is D_X^macro(C) = sum_i D_X(d_i) + I_X(C), where I_X(C) is the interaction correction; the total is summed over all five sectors {D, A, B, C, omega}.

name: Why interaction corrections matter
summary: The interaction term I_X is not perturbative but dominant in condensed systems: in crystals it locks mobility to zero, in superconductors the I_B term produces Cooper pairing and flux quantization; the bare sum alone describes only a non-interacting gas.

name: Macroscopic mobility
summary: Macroscopic mobility mu^macro(C) = (1/N) sum_i mu(d_i) + mu_int(C), the first component of D^macro, where the interaction term mu_int encodes collective resistance or facilitation of base-direction flow for the N-body configuration.

name: Macroscopic vorticity
summary: Macroscopic vorticity nu^macro(C) = (1/N) sum_i nu(d_i) + nu_int(C), the average rotational content plus collective circulation modes; in a superfluid nu^macro = 0 everywhere except at quantized vortex cores where topological charge concentrates.

name: Macroscopic compression
summary: Macroscopic compression kappa^macro(C) = (1/N) sum_i kappa(d_i) + kappa_int(C), measuring collective density variation; in a crystal kappa^macro approx 0 because the lattice enforces fixed density, while in a gas kappa^macro fluctuates freely.

name: Total topological charge
summary: Total topological charge theta^tot(C) = sum_i theta(d_i) is the additive, integer-valued sum of individual charges with no averaging and no interaction correction, since theta is a homotopy invariant immune to continuous deformation.

name: Topological charge conservation
summary: The total topological charge theta^tot(C) is conserved under any process that does not create or annihilate defect bundles: theta^tot(C_{n+1}) = theta^tot(C_n) for all primorial stages n, reflecting the homotopy invariance of winding numbers on T^2.

name: Clopen cylinder at depth n
summary: A clopen cylinder at primorial depth n is C_{n,a} = {x in Z-hat : x equiv a mod p_n#} for a in Z/p_n#Z; there are exactly p_n# such cylinders partitioning Z-hat, each simultaneously open and closed in the profinite topology.

name: Why clopen matters
summary: The clopen property means every cylinder is both an open neighborhood (accessible by local data) and a closed set (stable under limits), so no continuum approximation is needed; this structurally avoids the UV and IR divergences of continuum field theories.

name: Level-n defect functional
summary: The level-n defect functional delta_n[omega] maps clopen cylinders at depth n to R^3 x Z, assigning to each C_{n,a} the sum of defect components (mu, nu, kappa, theta) over all bundles whose addresses lie in that cylinder, plus interaction corrections.

name: Finitely specified at each depth
summary: At each primorial depth n, the functional delta_n is finitely specified: it assigns a 4-tuple to each of finitely many cylinders C_{n,0},...,C_{n,p_n#-1}; no infinite-dimensional function space is involved, a direct consequence of the profinite address structure.

name: Tower compatibility
summary: Tower compatibility: restriction of delta_{n+1} to the coarser partition recovers delta_n exactly, i.e., delta_{n+1}[omega]|_n(C_{n,a}) = sum_b delta_{n+1}[omega](C_{n+1, a+b*p_n#}) = delta_n[omega](C_{n,a}), ensuring coherence across primorial depths.

name: Ultrametric locality
summary: Interactions between defect bundles in different child cylinders at depth n+1 are suppressed by ultrametric distance >= p_n# in Z-hat, with the interaction kernel decaying faster than any polynomial; this ultrametric locality underwrites tower compatibility.

name: Universal defect functional
summary: The universal defect functional delta[omega] = projlim_n delta_n[omega] is the projective limit in the category of finitely additive measures on the clopen algebra of Z-hat valued in R^3 x Z, assigning a defect 4-tuple to every clopen subset U of Z-hat.

name: Existence and uniqueness of limit
summary: The projective limit delta[omega] exists and is unique: tower compatibility guarantees the system (delta_n)_{n>=1} is a compatible family of finitely additive measures on the directed system of clopen partitions, satisfying the universal property of the projective limit.

name: Sector additivity
summary: Sector additivity: the universal defect functional decomposes as delta[omega] = delta_D + delta_A + delta_B + delta_C + delta_omega, with each sector functional inheriting tower compatibility, because refinement maps commute with sector projection.

name: No continuum limit needed
summary: No continuum or thermodynamic limit (N,V -> infinity with N/V fixed) is needed in Category tau; the primorial tower already provides coherent infinite-resolution description, with delta[omega] exact at every finite depth n and the thermodynamic limit being the projective limit itself.

name: Defect tuple space
summary: The defect tuple space is D = R_{>=0} x R x R x Z, where the four factors correspond to (mu, nu, kappa, theta): mobility is non-negative, vorticity and compression are real-valued (signed), and topological charge is integer-valued.

name: Critical mobility threshold
summary: The critical mobility threshold mu_crit is the macroscopic mobility value at which the Euler budget ceases to hold; for mu <= mu_crit the budget is conserved, while for mu > mu_crit viscous dissipation breaks the budget and entropy production becomes non-negligible.

name: Crystal regime
summary: The crystal regime is the subset of D where mu < epsilon, |nu| < epsilon, |kappa| < epsilon, and theta = theta_0 (fixed integer); all three continuous components are nearly frozen and the topological charge is locked to a fixed value.

name: Glass regime
summary: The glass regime is the subset of D where mu < epsilon, |nu| < epsilon, |kappa| >= epsilon, and theta = theta_0; mobility and vorticity are frozen like a crystal, but compression remains unfrozen, allowing local density variations to persist.

name: Euler fluid regime
summary: The Euler fluid regime is the subset of D where 0 < mu <= mu_crit and the Euler budget constraint mu + nu + kappa + theta = B_0 holds; flow is inviscid with no entropy production, the tau-native analog of ideal fluid dynamics on the fiber T^2.

name: tau-Euler equation
summary: In the Euler fluid regime the macroscopic defect tuple evolves as d/dn (mu_n, nu_n, kappa_n, theta_n) = (f_mu, f_nu, f_kappa, 0) with f_mu + f_nu + f_kappa = 0 (budget conservation) and theta_n = theta_0 for all n (topological invariance).

name: Navier-Stokes regime
summary: The Navier-Stokes regime is the subset of D where mu > mu_crit; above the critical mobility threshold the Euler budget is violated, with viscous source terms (g_mu, g_nu, g_kappa) satisfying g_mu + g_nu + g_kappa != 0.

name: Navier-Stokes regularity
summary: Book III established positive regularity for tau-admissible fluid data (III.T25); the structural reason is compactness of the T^2 fiber, which prevents the defect-transport operator from developing singularities; the Clay Millennium NS problem link is noted.

name: MHD regime
summary: The MHD (magnetohydrodynamic) regime is the subset of D where nu >> mu and kappa is coupled to the B-sector; the system is rotating far more than translating, with compression locked to electromagnetic field gradients, describing stellar interiors and plasmas.

name: Why MHD lives in B-sector
summary: In Category tau the MHD coupling is automatic: the B-sector projection of the defect functional is the electromagnetic field content, and the nu-kappa interplay is the structural analog of magnetic pressure and tension forces; no additional coupling prescription is needed.

name: Plasma regime
summary: The plasma regime is the subset of D where mu, |nu|, |kappa| > mu_crit and theta is fluctuating (not globally fixed); topological charge varies across the configuration, representing the ionized, fully mobile state of matter.

name: Superfluid regime
summary: The superfluid regime is the subset of D where mu = mu_max, nu = 0 a.e. (vanishing vorticity except at isolated quantized vortex cores), kappa = 0 (incompressible), and theta in Z (quantized topological charge).

name: Superfluid vortex quantization
summary: In the superfluid regime every vortex core carries theta_core in Z \ {0}, and the total circulation around any loop enclosing k cores is oint nu = sum_j theta_{core,j} in Z; quantization follows from the integrality of theta on T^2.

name: Helium-4 and beyond
summary: In orthodox physics superfluid He-4 has quantized circulation h/m_{He}; in Category tau the quantization is structural from the integrality of theta on T^2, not from the ratio h/m; the Planck constant cancels against mass, leaving only the topological integer.

name: Superconductor regime
summary: The superconductor regime is the subset of D where B-sector mobility mu_B = mu_max, theta in Z (quantized), and magnetic flux Phi_B = theta * Phi_0 with flux quantum Phi_0 = hbar_tau/(2*theta_e); it combines maximal EM-sector mobility with flux quantization.

name: Flux quantization
summary: Flux quantization from topological charge: in the superconductor regime, the integrality of theta on the T^2 fiber implies Phi_B = n * Phi_0 (n in Z), since the magnetic flux through any loop equals the B-sector holonomy of the pair condensate.

name: Cooper pairing is topological
summary: Cooper pairing is topological in Category tau: two electron defect bundles with opposite momentum share a combined T^2 configuration whose theta is even; the phonon-mediated BCS attraction is the C-B cross-sector coupling in the defect functional.

name: Transitions as inequality crossings
summary: Every phase transition between the eight regimes is an inequality crossing in the defect tuple space D; no external order parameter (Landau theory) or symmetry-breaking mechanism is needed since the defect tuple itself is the order parameter.

name: Temperature as defect gradient
summary: The tau-temperature T_tau(C) = d delta[omega](C) / d S_def(C) is the gradient of the universal defect functional with respect to defect entropy, a derived quantity measuring the rate of defect-functional change when defect entropy varies at fixed refinement entropy.

name: Comparison with orthodox thermodynamics
summary: The tau-temperature inverts the orthodox definition 1/T = dS/dE: here T_tau = d delta / d S_def, reflecting that the defect functional delta[omega] is the fundamental object and temperature is what one reads when differentiating it.

name: Status of Boltzmann constant
summary: The Boltzmann constant k_B is an SI conversion factor, not an ontic tau-constant; it converts the dimensionless tau-temperature T_tau to kelvin-valued T_SI = (1/k_B) * Lift_therm(T_tau), paralleling how hbar and c convert other tau-native quantities to SI.

name: Parallel with hbar and c
summary: The status of k_B parallels hbar and c: all three are conversion factors between ontic tau-native quantities and their SI readouts; the Planck character converts via hbar, the base-fiber factor via c, and the defect-functional gradient via k_B.

name: Second law via defect functional
summary: Second law via defect functional: under propagation Phi_{n,n+1}, defect entropy S_def is non-increasing, refinement entropy S_ref is non-decreasing, total entropy S = S_def + S_ref is non-decreasing, and tau-temperature T_tau >= 0 always.

name: No negative temperatures
summary: In Category tau T_tau >= 0 always because the defect functional is a monotone readout of defect entropy with non-negative gradient; systems appearing to have negative temperature in orthodox thermodynamics have a readout functor that inverts the entropy gradient sign.

name: First-order phase transition
summary: A first-order phase transition at defect entropy S_0 is a discontinuity in the tau-temperature: lim_{S->S_0^-} T_tau(S) != lim_{S->S_0^+} T_tau(S), equivalently a jump discontinuity in d delta / d S_def at S_0.

name: Second-order phase transition
summary: A second-order phase transition at S_0 is a point where T_tau is continuous but its derivative dT_tau/dS_def is discontinuous, equivalently a jump in d^2 delta / d S_def^2; examples include the superconducting transition and superfluid lambda point.

name: Phase transition as regime crossing
summary: Every phase transition is an inequality crossing in D: first-order transitions correspond to the defect tuple jumping discontinuously between non-adjacent regime subsets, while second-order transitions correspond to the tuple arriving tangentially at a regime boundary.

name: Universality and critical exponents
summary: Critical exponents (alpha, beta, gamma, delta) of Landau-Ginzburg/Wilson-Fisher theory are readout-level quantities determined by the local geometry of the defect tuple space near the critical point; universality follows from the structural universality of the defect functional.

name: tau-Euler flow
summary: A tau-Euler flow is a sequence of tau-admissible configurations {d_n} satisfying: mobility bound mu(d_n) <= mu_crit, budget conservation mu_n + nu_n + kappa_n + theta_n = B_0, and propagation coherence d_{n+1} = Phi_{n,n+1}(d_n).

name: Kelvin theorem as budget law
summary: Kelvin's circulation theorem (oint v * dl conserved in inviscid barotropic flow) is the chart-level readout of the tau-Euler budget law; the four defect components play the role of the integrand and conservation holds because propagation preserves the budget surface.

name: tau-Navier-Stokes flow
summary: A tau-Navier-Stokes flow is a sequence {d_n} where mu(d_n) > mu_crit for some or all n, with viscous budget decay B_{n+1} - B_n proportional to mobility excess; the Euler budget is no longer conserved and entropy production occurs from viscous dissipation.

name: Finite at every primorial level
summary: At every primorial level n, the defect-tuple components satisfy |mu_n|, |nu_n|, |kappa_n|, |theta_n| <= M * Prim(n)^{1/2} for a uniform constant M; no component diverges because the propagation operator on compact T^2 at finite primorial levels is combinatorially bounded.

name: Viscosity coefficient eta_tau
summary: The tau-viscosity eta_tau is not a free parameter but is determined by the defect-functional geometry of the mobility-excess surface; through the readout functor R_mu it maps to the classical kinematic viscosity at the appropriate scale.

name: τ-NS Regularity on T² Fiber (C3 Defect Contractivity)
summary: Wave 11 upgrade: C3 defect contractivity proven for T² fiber. T² Laplacian discrete spectrum λ_{m,n}=m²+n² gives exponential decay per Fourier mode. Contraction factor κ = exp(−ν·λ₁₀) < 1. Regularity unconditional within τ-admissible class on T². Scope: tau-effective for T² fiber; Clay Millennium Problem gap (T² → ℝ³) honestly acknowledged.

name: Honesty about the Clay problem
summary: Honest scope assessment: regularity is unconditional within the tau-admissible class, but closing the gap to the Clay Millennium Problem's Schwartz-class data requires proving representability or reformulating the Clay problem in tau-admissible terms.

name: Superfluid regime
summary: A tau-admissible configuration is in the superfluid regime if mu is maximal (free base-direction translation), nu = 0 except at isolated vortex cores with non-trivial winding around T^2 cycles, and kappa = 0 (incompressible bulk).

name: Quantized circulation
summary: In the superfluid regime the circulation around any closed loop C on T^2 is quantized: oint v_s * dl = (2*pi*hbar_tau/m) * theta_C with theta_C in Z, where theta_C is the winding number of the boundary character around C.

name: Superconductor regime
summary: A tau-admissible configuration is in the superconductor regime if B-sector mobility mu_B is maximal, topological charge theta_B in Z is quantized, and magnetic flux Phi_B = n*Phi_0 (n in Z) with flux quantum Phi_0 = h/(2e).

name: BCS gap as spectral gap
summary: The BCS energy gap Delta_BCS is the B-sector spectral gap of the defect functional delta[omega]: the minimum cost of unpacking a paired defect bundle into two isolated defects with nonzero net topological charge; the BCS formula is its chart-level readout.

name: Crystal regime
summary: A tau-admissible configuration is in the crystal regime if mu approx 0 (atoms locked), nu approx 0 (no rotation), kappa approx 0 (rigid lattice), and theta forms a repeating periodic pattern on T^2 with coherent long-range order.

name: Crystal symmetry from torus subgroups
summary: The 17 wallpaper groups and 230 space groups of crystallography are discrete subgroups of the torus symmetry T^2 = S^1 x S^1 (extended to T^2 x tau^1 for 3D); crystal symmetry classification is classification of admissible periodic theta-patterns on T^2.

name: Glass regime
summary: A tau-admissible configuration is in the glass regime if mu approx 0 (locked), nu approx 0 (locked), kappa > 0 (compression unfrozen, local density variations persist), and theta is fixed but aperiodic (no repeating topological charge pattern).

name: Glass transition not a true phase transition
summary: The glass transition is not a true phase transition in the defect-tuple framework: mu drops continuously to near zero with no sharp boundary, latent heat, or divergent susceptibility; it is a kinetic arrest where the system falls out of equilibrium.

name: Quasicrystal regime
summary: A tau-admissible configuration is in the quasicrystal regime if all four components (mu, nu, kappa, theta) are frozen and the topological charge pattern theta has incommensurate winding numbers around the two circles of T^2 (irrational ratio w_gamma/w_eta).

name: Penrose tilings on the torus
summary: Penrose tilings arise as a special case of incommensurate torus windings: the projection angle is arctan(w_gamma/w_eta), the higher-dimensional lattice is the CR-address lattice Lambda on T^2, and fivefold symmetry emerges when the winding ratio involves the golden ratio.

name: First-order phase transition
summary: A first-order phase transition (ch53 formulation) is a transition where one or more defect-tuple components undergo a discontinuous jump as a control parameter crosses an inequality boundary; examples include melting (mu jumps from ~0 to >0).

name: Second-order phase transition
summary: A second-order (continuous) phase transition is a transition where all defect-tuple components are continuous but one or more of their derivatives with respect to a control parameter diverge at the inequality boundary; examples include the normal-to-superconductor transition.

name: Defect tuple as universal order parameter
summary: The defect tuple D = (mu, nu, kappa, theta) is simultaneously the state variable and the universal order parameter for all phase transitions: components are discontinuous in first-order transitions, and their derivatives diverge in second-order transitions.

name: Universality from sector structure
summary: Universality classes in orthodox statistical mechanics (determined by dimension and order-parameter symmetry) correspond in the defect-tuple framework to which sectors participate: a B-sector-only transition has U(1) symmetry, B+C transitions give QCD deconfinement class.

name: Melting sequence monotone mobility
summary: The six non-topological regimes are ordered by monotonically increasing macroscopic mobility: mu_crystal <= mu_quasi <= mu_glass < mu_Euler < mu_NS < mu_plasma; the topological regimes (superfluid, superconductor) are reached orthogonally by lowering the gradient.

name: Topological branch
summary: A topological branch of the defect spectrum is a regime with nonzero maximal mobility, zero bulk vorticity nu_bulk = 0, and quantized topological charge theta in Z (or theta_B in Z for the B-sector branch); superfluids and superconductors live on topological branches.

name: Fiber-base factorization
summary: The universal defect functional on tau^3 = tau^1 x_f T^2 factorizes as delta[omega]_{tau^3} = delta[omega]_{T^2} tensor delta[omega]_{tau^1}, with the fiber encoding sectors A, B, C, omega and the base encoding sector D; the only coupling passes through the omega-sector Kirchhoff law.

name: Why the separation is clean
summary: The fiber-base factorization is exact by axiom K5 and the lemniscate L; the only fiber-base coupling passes through the omega-sector Kirchhoff law, meaning no backreaction of gravity on QM -- the two are linked only through Higgs/mass, exactly as observed in laboratory physics.

name: Fiber-level physics is complete
summary: At the close of Part VII, every tau-admissible phenomenon on the fiber T^2 is classified: single particles (Part VI), quantum mechanics (Part III), gauge forces (Parts IV-V), many-body states and condensed matter (Part VII); Book V inherits the framework for the base tau^1.

name: The base is not the rest
summary: The base tau^1 has its own rich structure: D-sector with coupling 1 - iota_tau, temporal complement kappa(A;1) + kappa(D;1) = 1, and full cosmological phenomenology; fiber and base are equally structured, equally essential -- the two components of tau^3.

name: Tower-natural transformation
summary: A tower-natural transformation eta: F => G between sector functors F, G: tau^1 -> tau^3|_{T^2} is a family {eta_n: F[n] -> G[n]} in the boundary holonomy algebra H_partial satisfying eta_{n+1} circ Phi^F = Phi^G circ eta_n for all tower levels n >= 1, encoding compatibility with propagation.

name: Noether theorem as corollary
